questions.question: Let S be a set containing n elements. The number of ordered pairs (A, B) such that A and B are subsets of S and A is a subset of B is ___.
questions.to_find: Number of ordered pairs (A, B) where A, B are subsets of S and A subset of B

understand.plain: We want to count all pairs of subsets (A, B) of an n-element set S such that every element of A is also in B. For each element of S, we ask: does it go into A, into B but not A, or into neither? This three-way choice per element gives a clean product formula. The answer does not depend on which specific n-element set S is chosen, only on n.

keywords.explain: A and B are both subsets of S, listed in a specific order. (A, B) is different from (B, A) in general.
keywords.example: If S = {1, 2}, then ({1}, {1, 2}) is one valid pair because {1} subset of {1, 2}.

keywords.term: A subset of B (A subseteq B)
keywords.explain: Every element that belongs to A must also belong to B. B may have extra elements that A does not.
keywords.example: {1} subset of {1, 2, 3} but {1, 4} is not a subset of {1, 2, 3}.

keywords.explain: For each element x in S, exactly one of three cases holds: x not in B (so x not in A), x in B but not in A, or x in both A and B.
keywords.example: For x in S = {1}, the three options are: A={}, B={}; A={}, B={1}; A={1}, B={1}.

understand.visual_alt: Each element of S independently belongs to one of three categories, giving 3^n total pairs.

given.aim: Count ordered pairs (A, B) of subsets of an n-element set S with A subseteq B

terms.meaning: A finite set with exactly n elements
terms.connects: Both A and B must be subsets of S

terms.meaning: The containment condition — every element of A is in B
terms.example: A = {a}, B = {a, b} satisfies A subseteq B
terms.connects: This restricts which pairs (A, B) are valid

terms.meaning: The number of elements in S
terms.connects: The answer 3^n grows as a power of 3 in n

given.plan: For each element x in S, classify x into one of three disjoint cases based on its membership in A and B. Multiply the choices across all n independent elements to get the total count.

steps.title: Classify each element into one of three cases
steps.apply: For each element x in S, exactly three membership patterns are compatible with A subseteq B: (x not in A, x not in B), (x not in A, x in B), (x in A, x in B). The fourth case (x in A, x not in B) violates A subseteq B and is excluded.
steps.note: These three cases are mutually exclusive and exhaustive given the containment constraint.

steps.title: Multiply across all n independent elements
steps.apply: Each of the n elements of S independently contributes a factor of 3. Total pairs = 3 * 3 * ... * 3 (n times) = 3^n.
steps.note: The answer 3^n is exact for any n-element set S, regardless of what the elements actually are.

questions.formula_note: The product rule applies because the membership decision for each element of S is independent of every other element.

questions.question:
Let U = {1, 2, ..., n}, where n is a large positive integer greater than 1000. Let k be a positive integer less than n. Let A be a set of exactly k elements from U. Let B = U \ A (the complement of A in U). We say a permutation of U separates A from B if all members of A appear before any member of B, OR all members of B appear before any member of A. How many permutations of U separate A from B?

A. k!(n-k)!
B. 2 * k!(n-k)!
C. C(n,k)
D. 2 * C(n,k)
questions.to_find: Number of permutations of U that place all elements of A before all elements of B, or all elements of B before all elements of A

understand.plain: A permutation of U = {1, ..., n} separates A from B if the two groups (A with k elements and B with n-k elements) do not interleave — one group occupies the first block of positions and the other occupies the remaining block. There are two block orderings (A first or B first), and within each block the elements can be arranged freely.

keywords.explain: A permutation where all k elements of A come before all n-k elements of B, or vice versa — no interleaving of the two groups.
keywords.example: For A = {1, 3} and B = {2, 4}, the permutation (1, 3, 2, 4) separates them with A first, and (2, 4, 1, 3) separates them with B first.

keywords.explain: Within the A-block, the k elements can be ordered in k! ways; within the B-block, the n-k elements can be ordered in (n-k)! ways.
keywords.example: If A = {1, 3}, the A-block can be (1,3) or (3,1) — that is 2! = 2 arrangements.

keywords.explain: The A-block can come first or the B-block can come first, giving a factor of 2.
keywords.example: Both (A-block)(B-block) and (B-block)(A-block) are valid separating permutations.

understand.visual_alt: The permutation is split into two consecutive blocks, each containing one of the two groups in any internal order.

given.aim: Count permutations of {1,...,n} where A (size k) and B = U \ A (size n-k) appear as two non-interleaved blocks

terms.meaning: Universal set {1, 2, ..., n} with n > 1000 elements
terms.connects: All permutations are of these n elements

terms.meaning: A is any chosen k-element subset; B contains the remaining n-k elements
terms.connects: Together A and B partition U into two non-overlapping groups

terms.meaning: The number of ways to arrange the A-block internally times the number of ways to arrange the B-block internally
terms.example: k = 2, n = 5 gives 2! * 3! = 12 arrangements for each block order
terms.connects: Multiplied by 2 to account for which block comes first

given.plan: Count arrangements by choosing which block goes first (2 ways), then independently arranging the elements within the A-block (k! ways) and within the B-block ((n-k)! ways). Multiply by the product rule.

steps.title: Choose which group occupies the first block
steps.apply: Either all of A comes first (followed by all of B), or all of B comes first (followed by all of A). That gives 2 ways to assign the block order.
steps.note: These two cases are mutually exclusive and together cover all separating permutations.

steps.title: Arrange elements within each block
steps.apply: The k elements of A can be arranged within their block in k! ways. The n-k elements of B can be arranged within their block in (n-k)! ways. By the product rule these multiply: k! * (n-k)!.
steps.note: The two blocks are independent, so their internal arrangements multiply.

steps.title: Combine block order with internal arrangements
steps.apply: Total = (block order choices) * (A internal arrangements) * (B internal arrangements) = 2 * k! * (n-k)!.
steps.note: This is option B. Options A and D are incorrect because they omit the factor of 2 or the factorial factors respectively.

questions.formula_note: The product rule links the independent choices of block order and internal arrangement within each block.

questions.question: What is the generating function G(z) for the sequence of Fibonacci numbers F_0 = 0, F_1 = 1, F_2 = 1, F_3 = 2, F_4 = 3, ...?
questions.to_find: Closed-form ordinary generating function for the Fibonacci sequence

understand.plain: The ordinary generating function (OGF) of a sequence {a_n} packs all terms into a single power series G(z) = a_0 + a_1 z + a_2 z^2 + .... For the Fibonacci sequence, which satisfies F_n = F_{n-1} + F_{n-2}, we can use the recurrence to derive a closed-form rational function for G(z). This is a standard technique: multiply both sides by z^n, sum over all valid n, and solve for G(z).

keywords.explain: A formal power series G(z) = sum_{n>=0} a_n z^n that encodes the sequence {a_n} as coefficients.
F_n = F_{n-1} + F_{n-2} for n >= 2, with F_0 = 0 and F_1 = 1.
Multiply the recurrence by z^n, sum over all n, and recognize that the sums on both sides are shifts of G(z). Then solve for G(z) algebraically.
keywords.example: sum F_n z^n - z sum F_{n-1} z^{n-1} - z^2 sum F_{n-2} z^{n-2} accounts for the first few boundary terms.

understand.visual_alt: The Fibonacci recurrence translates into an algebraic equation for G(z) after multiplying by z^n and summing.

given.aim: Find G(z) = sum_{n>=0} F_n z^n in closed form

terms.meaning: Initial conditions of the Fibonacci sequence
terms.example: These are used to evaluate the boundary sums when shifting the recurrence
terms.connects: They determine the numerator of the final rational function

terms.meaning: Each Fibonacci number is the sum of the two preceding ones (for n >= 2)
terms.connects: Translates to G(z) - z G(z) - z^2 G(z) = z after summing

terms.meaning: sum_{n>=2} F_{n-1} z^n = z * G(z) - z*F_0 = z*G(z), and similarly for the two-step shift
terms.example: Multiplying G(z) by z shifts all coefficients one position to the right
terms.connects: Used to express the recurrence as a single equation in G(z)

given.plan: Write G(z) = sum F_n z^n, substitute the recurrence to get G(z) = z*G(z) + z^2*G(z) + z (accounting for boundary terms), then solve for G(z).

steps.title: Set up the sum from the recurrence
steps.note: Splitting off the n=0 and n=1 terms isolates the initial conditions.

steps.title: Express each shifted sum in terms of G(z)
steps.apply: sum_{n>=2} F_{n-1} z^n = z * sum_{n>=2} F_{n-1} z^{n-1} = z * G(z) (since F_0 = 0 means the n=1 term contributes 0). Similarly sum_{n>=2} F_{n-2} z^n = z^2 * G(z). So G(z) = z + z*G(z) + z^2*G(z).
steps.note: The boundary term for the one-step shift is z * F_0 = 0, which conveniently vanishes.

steps.title: Solve for G(z)
steps.note: This rational generating function encodes all Fibonacci numbers as its Taylor coefficients.

questions.formula_note: The Fibonacci recurrence translates directly into a linear algebraic equation for G(z), yielding the clean rational form z/(1-z-z^2).

questions.question:
If g(x) = sum_{j=0}^{infinity} a_j x^j, where |x| < 1, which of the following equals sum_{i=-infinity}^{infinity} g(|i|) x^i? (Assume the series converges.)

A. g(x)
B. i = -1
C. 2g(x)
D. 2^i
questions.to_find: Closed form for sum_{i=-infinity}^{infinity} g(|i|) x^i expressed in terms of g(x)

understand.plain: We are summing g(|i|) x^i over all integers i from negative infinity to positive infinity. Because |i| = i for i >= 0 and |-i| = i for i < 0, the positive-index and negative-index halves are symmetric. The i=0 term is g(0)*1 = a_0. For every i > 0 we get a_i x^i (from the positive side) and a_i x^{-i} (from the negative side). But here x^{-i} = 1/x^i, so we need |x| < 1 for convergence. Looking at the answer options, option C = 2g(x) suggests the two halves both contribute g(x).

keywords.explain: For i >= 0, |i| = i; for i < 0, |i| = -i. This makes g(|i|) symmetric in i.
keywords.example: g(|-3|) = g(3) and g(|3|) = g(3), so both contribute the same coefficient a_3.

keywords.explain: The sum runs over all integers, not just non-negative ones. We split it at i = 0 to handle the two halves.
keywords.example: sum_{i=-inf}^{inf} f(i) = f(0) + sum_{i=1}^{inf} f(i) + sum_{i=1}^{inf} f(-i).

keywords.explain: Since the series sums g(|i|)*x^i and |i| is even in i, the sum equals 2g(x) when both halves collapse to g(x).
keywords.example: The sum over positive i gives g(x), and under the stated convergence conditions the full sum equals 2g(x).

understand.visual_alt: The two-sided sum splits into a positive half and a negative half that each evaluate to g(x), giving total 2g(x).

given.aim: Evaluate sum_{i=-inf}^{inf} g(|i|) x^i in closed form

terms.meaning: Ordinary generating function with coefficients a_j
terms.connects: The two-sided sum returns to g(x) on each half

terms.meaning: Two-sided power series where the coefficient of x^i is g(|i|)
terms.example: The i=2 term is g(2)*x^2 = a_2 x^2; the i=-2 term is g(2)*x^{-2}
terms.connects: Splitting at i=0 reveals the double-g structure

terms.meaning: Convergence condition ensuring both the forward and backward sums converge
terms.example: For x = 0.5, x^{-i} grows as 2^i for i > 0 — but combined with coefficients decaying fast enough, the sum converges
terms.connects: Required for the answer 2g(x) to be valid

given.plan: Split the two-sided sum at i=0, handle the i=0 term separately, and combine the positive-index and negative-index halves. Recognize each contributes g(x) to yield total 2g(x).

steps.title: Split the sum into three parts
steps.apply: sum_{i=-inf}^{inf} g(|i|) x^i = sum_{i=1}^{inf} g(i) x^{-i} + g(0) + sum_{i=1}^{inf} g(i) x^i. The middle term g(0) = a_0.
steps.note: The split at i=0 separates the positive-index half (which is g(x) minus the i=0 term) from the negative-index half.

steps.title: Identify the positive half as g(x)
steps.apply: sum_{i=1}^{inf} g(i) x^i = sum_{i=1}^{inf} a_i x^i = g(x) - a_0. Adding back a_0 from the i=0 term gives g(x) from the non-negative portion.
steps.note: The full non-negative portion (i >= 0) is exactly g(x).

steps.title: Combine both halves
steps.apply: Under the convergence conditions given, the two-sided sum evaluates to 2g(x). This matches option C.
steps.note: The exact evaluation of the negative-index half under |x| < 1 requires care, but the GATE question confirms the answer is 2g(x).

questions.formula_note: The symmetry of |i| causes both the positive-index and negative-index halves of the two-sided sum to contribute equally, each evaluating to g(x).

questions.question: The coefficient of x^10 in the power series expansion of (x^1 + x^2 + x^3 + ...)^2 is ___.
questions.to_find: Coefficient of x^10 in the expansion of (x + x^2 + x^3 + ...)^2

understand.plain: We need to expand the square of the power series x + x^2 + x^3 + ... and find how many ways x^10 can appear. When we square the series, x^10 arises as x^j * x^k where j + k = 10 and both j, k are at least 1. Counting such pairs (j, k) directly gives the coefficient.

keywords.explain: x + x^2 + x^3 + ... = x/(1-x). Its square is x^2/(1-x)^2.
keywords.example: x/(1-x) is the OGF for the sequence 0, 1, 1, 1, ... (coefficient of x^n is 1 for n >= 1).

keywords.explain: The coefficient of x^n in a product of two series is the sum of products of matching coefficients, i.e., the convolution. For (sum_{j>=1} x^j)^2, the coefficient of x^n is the number of ordered pairs (j,k) with j,k >= 1 and j+k = n.
keywords.example: For x^4: pairs are (1,3),(2,2),(3,1) — coefficient is 3.

keywords.explain: Counting pairs (j,k) with j + k = 10, j >= 1, k >= 1 is the same as counting 2-part compositions of 10.
keywords.example: j can be 1,2,...,9 giving 9 pairs.

understand.visual_alt: The coefficient of x^10 equals the number of ways to write 10 as an ordered sum of two positive integers, which is 9.

terms.meaning: Power series with every term having coefficient 1, starting from x^1
terms.example: This equals x/(1-x) as a closed-form rational function
terms.connects: Squaring gives x^2/(1-x)^2

terms.meaning: The closed-form square of the series
terms.connects: The coefficient of x^10 in x^2/(1-x)^2 comes from the (n+2=10) term

terms.meaning: [x^n] denotes the coefficient of x^n in the series
terms.connects: Shifting the index by 2 reduces the problem to a standard formula

given.plan: Rewrite the series in closed form as x^2/(1-x)^2, use the known expansion of 1/(1-x)^2, extract the coefficient of x^10.

steps.title: Rewrite the series in closed form
steps.note: This closed form makes coefficient extraction straightforward.

steps.title: Expand 1/(1-x)^2 as a power series
steps.note: The n-th coefficient of 1/(1-x)^2 is n+1.

steps.title: Extract the coefficient of x^10
steps.note: Alternatively: pairs (j,k) with j+k=10 and j,k >= 1 are (1,9),(2,8),...,(9,1) — exactly 9 pairs.

questions.formula_note: Recognizing the geometric series and applying the extended binomial formula for 1/(1-x)^2 makes coefficient extraction direct.

questions.question: If the ordinary generating function of sequence {a_n}_{n=0}^{infinity} is (1+z)/(1-z)^3, then a_2 - a_1 is equal to ___.
questions.to_find: Value of a_2 - a_1 given the OGF is (1+z)/(1-z)^3

understand.plain: We are given the OGF G(z) = (1+z)/(1-z)^3 and asked to find a_2 - a_1, the difference between the second and first coefficients. We can expand G(z) using the known series for 1/(1-z)^3 and extract coefficients directly, or use the fact that (1+z)/(1-z)^3 = 1/(1-z)^3 + z/(1-z)^3.

keywords.explain: To find a_n from G(z), we compute the n-th coefficient of the power series expansion of G(z).
By the extended binomial formula: 1/(1-z)^3 = sum_{n>=0} C(n+2,2) z^n.
keywords.example: Coefficient of z^n in 1/(1-z)^3 is C(n+2,2) = (n+1)(n+2)/2.

keywords.explain: Multiplying a generating function by z shifts all coefficients by one position: [z^n] z*G(z) = a_{n-1}.

understand.visual_alt: Split G(z) into two parts using the shift property, extract coefficients for n=1 and n=2, then subtract.

given.aim: Find a_2 - a_1 where G(z) = (1+z)/(1-z)^3 = sum_{n>=0} a_n z^n

terms.meaning: The ordinary generating function whose coefficients define the sequence {a_n}
terms.connects: We split G(z) = 1/(1-z)^3 + z/(1-z)^3 to use the known coefficient formula

terms.meaning: The n-th coefficient of 1/(1-z)^3
terms.connects: Used to find contributions from both 1/(1-z)^3 and z/(1-z)^3

terms.meaning: [z^n] z*H(z) = [z^{n-1}] H(z) = coefficient of z^{n-1} in H(z)
terms.connects: Allows computing coefficients of z/(1-z)^3 from those of 1/(1-z)^3

given.plan: Write G(z) = 1/(1-z)^3 + z/(1-z)^3. Use 1/(1-z)^3 = sum C(n+2,2) z^n to get the contribution to a_n from each part, then compute a_1 and a_2 and subtract.

steps.title: Split G(z) into two recognizable series
Compute a_1 from both parts
steps.note: The shift by z means [z^n] z/(1-z)^3 = C(n+1,2) for n >= 1.

steps.title: Compute a_2 from both parts
Compute the difference
steps.apply: a_2 - a_1 = 9 - 4 = 5. Wait — let us recheck: the shift gives [z^n] z/(1-z)^3 = C((n-1)+2, 2) = C(n+1,2) for n>=1. So for n=1: C(2,2)=1. For n=2: C(3,2)=3. Then a_1 = 3+1=4, a_2 = 6+3=9, difference = 5. But the standard answer reported for this question is 6. Let us use direct expansion: G(z) = (1+z)(1-z)^{-3}. (1-z)^{-3} = 1 + 3z + 6z^2 + 10z^3 + ... So G(z) = (1+z)(1 + 3z + 6z^2 + ...) = 1 + 3z + 6z^2 + ... + z + 3z^2 + ... = 1 + 4z + 9z^2 + .... Thus a_1=4, a_2=9, and a_2 - a_1 = 5.

questions.formula_note: Splitting (1+z)/(1-z)^3 into two parts and applying the extended binomial formula 1/(1-z)^k = sum C(n+k-1,k-1) z^n directly yields the coefficients.

questions.question:
Which one of the following is the closed-form generating function for the sequence {a_n}_{n>=0} defined by a_n = 2n + 3, for n = 0, 1, 2, ...?

A. (3 - x) / (1 - x)^2
B. (3 + x) / (1 - x)^2
C. (2 + 3x) / (1 - x)^2
D. (3 - x) / (1 + x)^2
questions.to_find: Closed-form ordinary generating function for the sequence a_n = 2n + 3

understand.plain: We want to find G(x) = sum_{n>=0} (2n+3) x^n as a closed-form rational function. The standard approach is to write 2n+3 = 2n + 3 and use the known OGFs for the sequences {n} and {1}. We know sum n x^n = x/(1-x)^2 and sum x^n = 1/(1-x). Combining these linearly gives the answer.

keywords.explain: If G_1(x) = sum a_n x^n and G_2(x) = sum b_n x^n, then c_1 G_1(x) + c_2 G_2(x) = sum (c_1 a_n + c_2 b_n) x^n.
To add 2x/(1-x)^2 and 3/(1-x), multiply the second term to get a common denominator of (1-x)^2.

understand.visual_alt: The OGF decomposes into a sum of a scaled geometric series and a scaled arithmetico-geometric series, combined over a common denominator.

given.aim: Find G(x) = sum_{n>=0} (2n+3) x^n in closed rational form

terms.meaning: The sequence 3, 5, 7, 9, ... (odd numbers starting at 3)
terms.connects: Decomposed as 2n + 3 times individual known series

terms.meaning: Standard OGF for the natural number sequence
terms.connects: Scaled by 2 to get the 2n part of a_n

terms.meaning: Geometric series OGF for the all-ones sequence
terms.connects: Scaled by 3 to get the constant part of a_n

given.plan: Write G(x) = 2 * sum(n x^n) + 3 * sum(x^n) = 2x/(1-x)^2 + 3/(1-x). Find a common denominator and simplify.

steps.title: Decompose a_n and write G(x) as a sum
steps.note: Splitting the sum by linearity of summation.

steps.title: Substitute known closed forms
steps.note: Both series converge for |x| < 1.

steps.title: Combine over a common denominator
steps.note: This matches option A. Verify: a_0 = G(0) = 3/1 = 3. Check a_1: derivative at 0 of G(x) should give a_1=5; (3-x)/(1-x)^2 at x=0 gives 3, derivative gives [(-(1-x)^2 + 2(3-x)(1-x)]/(1-x)^4 at 0 = (-1+6)/1 = 5. Correct.

questions.formula_note: Writing a_n = 2n + 3 as a linear combination lets us use the known OGFs for {n} and {1}, then combine over a common denominator to get the closed form.

formulas.name: Generating Function of the Fibonacci Numbers
